lines.text: The front steps of Crestwood High are wider than your old school's whole parking lot. You've been standing here for two minutes. Maybe three.
Okay. New plan: I make zero friends, I make zero enemies, I graduate invisible. Perfectly reasonable life goal.
No te achiques, Abuela used to say. Don't make yourself smaller.
Cool. Great. Definitely not planning to do the exact opposite of that in the next ninety seconds.
Under her breath, one more run-through: "Hi, I'm Sol. Sol. Hi, I'm—"
Just get the first five minutes right. That's the whole plan. Five minutes.
She lets out a breath and pushes through the doors.
The hallway swallows her — lockers, noise, a current of people who already know exactly where they're going.
Okay. This is fine. This is a hallway. You've walked through hallways before.
Okay, new girl — I'm calling it, you need a table. Best one in the building. Might be biased. Still true.
lines.action: jogs up, touches her shoulder briefly

lines.text: Wow. Four minutes in and I've already got a reputation.
Honestly? That's basically a school record for how long people go without hearing about Priya's table.
Across the hall, on a bench under the trophy case, someone with a sketchbook is very deliberately not looking up at anyone.
Something about that stillness snags her. She knows that particular kind of trying-not-to-be-noticed.
Oh — that's Alex. Keeps to themself. Kind of always has, since like, forever.
Anyway. C'mon, before the good seats are gone—
The table by the window is loud before you even reach it — laughter that seems to exist independent of any joke.
So you're the new one.
lines.action: doesn't look up from her phone

lines.text: Jordan told us nothing about you, which is very on-brand for him.
Hey—
I'm kidding. Mostly.
Sit. You're making the table look unbalanced.
Wouldn't want that. Structural integrity and everything.
Oh, she's funny. Jordan, why didn't you lead with that.
I— did? I feel like I did.
Someone at the table gestures out the window, toward the courtyard.
Oh my god, look — Sketchbook's out here having a whole art residency. By himself. Again.
The table laughs. Priya's eyes flick to you — checking, just for a second, whether you're in on it.
Alex trails a half-step behind you, sketchbook hugged to their chest like a shield.
Alex, right? Cool, sit wherever, we don't really do assigned seats. Except Priya has assigned seats.
I have a system. It's different.
Alex sits at the very edge of the bench, spine straight, sketchbook still closed in their lap.
This is fine.
Nobody asked if it was fine, but okay.
Okay but anyway—did you guys hear Coach Marsh already gave a pop quiz? Day one. Iconic, honestly.
The bench under the trophy case is emptier than any table in the cafeteria, and somehow louder in your ears.
Hey. Mind if I sit?
It's a free country.
That's not a yes, by the way. It's also not a no.
I'll take it.
A long quiet. Alex's pencil keeps moving. You watch it more than you mean to.
You don't have to sit here. I'm not, like, a project.
I know. I just didn't feel like performing 'new kid' for a whole table today.
...Huh.
lines.action: pencil stops for half a second

lines.text: I'm new too. Moved here in June. Nobody really—
Anyway. It's fine.
Nobody really what?
Nobody really notices you if you don't make noise. Turns out that's easier than I thought it'd be.
I don't know. Feels like a full-time job, the not-making-noise thing.
...Yeah. Overtime, no benefits.
Across the cafeteria, Jordan glances over — not annoyed, just curious, like he's recalculating something.
This guy's having a rough day.
lines.action: turns sketchbook slightly, showing a panel of a kid mid-fall

lines.text: Relatable.
Ms. Chen stands at the front with a clipboard, and the room already knows better than to ask questions before she's done.
Semester project. Pairs. I'm assigning them, I don't take requests, and I definitely don't take them after the fact.
Groans ripple. Ms. Chen waits a beat too long, lets it die on its own.
Sol and Alex. That's the pairing.
Across the room, Alex is already zipping their bag shut, shoulders squared, bracing for a complaint that hasn't come yet.
Oh, hey, no, that's actually good, Alex is really talented, honestly.
Priya and I got stuck together. Pray for me.
I heard that.
It's fine.
Books between you, half a poster board unrolled, the project mostly an excuse at this point.
So my dad got a job here in June. We packed in like four days.
Four days? That's insane.
Yeah, well. He does that. Third move in two years.
This guy—
—he's had a rough couple years too. Coincidentally.
Is 'this guy' doing okay?
...Getting there. Slowly.
Anyway. Drawing's just — it's a thing I can control when everything else is packed in a box.
My abuela used to say something like that. 'No te achiques.' Don't make yourself smaller.
I don't know why it's easier to hear than to do.
...Yeah. That tracks.
lines.action: looks at her properly for the first time

lines.text: Something loosens between you — small, but real.
He's got a good arm, terrible aim.
Kind of like you, probably, with whatever sport you pretend to be good at.
Rude. Accurate. But rude.

lines.emotion: a real laugh, half-late but real

lines.text: Library table, two chairs pushed exactly as far apart as they can be and still share a book.
Okay, so I did the intro section. You said you'd have the visuals?
Yeah. Done.
lines.action: slides a folder across, doesn't look up

lines.text: These are really good, actually.
Thanks.
A silence stretches. Not comfortable. Not hostile. Just closed.
So, um — how's it going? Like, in general.
Fine.
Cool. Cool, cool.
You've lived here long?
Not really.
Alex checks the clock, then zips their bag — the tell that a conversation is ending whether or not it's actually done.
I should get going.
Oh — yeah. Okay.
There's a door here. You just don't know the combination yet.
Your phone buzzes on the desk, project papers spread out around it.
ok i know tonight's bad timing but EVERYONE'S gonna be there tonight only, i'll literally die, come for like an hour 😭😭
You promised Alex tonight. You also, apparently, promised your own social survival to a group chat, somehow, without noticing.
no for real sol it's not the same without you, come onnnn
Decision time.
lines.action: twists ring, even though there's no one to see it

lines.text: Alex's living room, project spread across the coffee table. The door was answered almost before you finished knocking.
You came.
lines.emotion: flat, but the eyes give it away

lines.text: I said I would.
People say a lot of things.
...Cool. Come in, I guess.
The project comes together faster than either of you expected — like the work was easier once the worry about whether you'd show up was gone.
Your section's the weak link, by the way.
Wow. Harsh. Accurate, but harsh.
...Ha. I'm kidding. Mostly.
Something settles into place that has nothing to do with the poster board.
The party is loud, bright, exactly the scene you pictured — and somehow you spend half of it checking your phone.
See? Told you. Everyone's here, it's honestly perfect.
Look who made it. Didn't think you had it in you to actually show up places.
Wouldn't miss it.
Somewhere across town, Alex is probably still waiting. Or worse, not waiting anymore.
Next day, hallway. Alex is polite. Professional. A door that used to be cracked open is shut all the way now.
Hey — I'm really sorry about last night, I meant to—
It's fine. We got it done.
See you in class.
"It's fine" has never once meant fine. You'd know. You've said it yourself.
You make it to the party for exactly the amount of time you promised — no more.
Wait, you're leaving already? Come on—
I know, I know, I've got somewhere to be. Next time, full night, promise.
Sure you do.
You run six blocks to Alex's, arriving out of breath, twenty minutes later than you said.
...You're late.
I know. I'm sorry. I wanted to at least — I didn't want to just not show.
Alex studies her for a second too long, deciding something.
...Come in. We've got a lot left.
You could've just texted that you weren't coming. Most people do.
I'm not most people.
...Yeah, okay. Sit down, we're behind.
Ms. Chen taps the clipboard against her palm. The room quiets before she even says a word.
Sol. Alex. You're up.
Alex gathers the poster board, hands not quite steady. Every eye in the room shifts toward you both.
...Okay. Here we go.
Here we go.
Ms. Chen settles into the back of the room, pen ready, waiting.
Applause, more real than polite. Ms. Chen gives one of her rare full nods.
You covered a lot of ground up there. Good balance. Both of you.
...Thanks. For the—
lines.action: doesn't quite finish, doesn't quite meet her eyes

lines.text: You did the work. I just said it out loud.
Walking out, it hits you — you didn't have to become someone else to end up here. You just had to stop shrinking.
Alex falls into step beside her, sketchbook under one arm, and doesn't peel off toward some other direction. Not today.
The applause comes, polite and on schedule. Ms. Chen makes a note in tiny, precise handwriting you can't read from here.
Technically solid. You covered the material.
Some of it wasn't yours to cover.
lines.action: looking at Alex, then at player

lines.text: Alex is already at the door before the bell even finishes, sketchbook clutched tight, not looking back.
Alex, wait—
The hallway swallows them before you get the rest of the sentence out.
You got the grade. You're not sure you got anything else.
The class actually claps for real this time — the surprised kind, the kind Alex clearly wasn't expecting.
Good work. Both of you.
I noticed what you did — stepping back. That's harder than people think. Good.
...That actually went okay.
Better than okay. You were kind of great up there.
...Don't make it weird.
Not bad, Sketchbook.
You too, I guess.
It's not a big moment. It doesn't need to be.

options.text: That sounds great, count me in!
Thanks! Mind if that person joins us too?
I think I'm going to go say hi to them first.

a.text: That's what I like to hear. Come on—
a.action: touches her shoulder, steering her toward the cafeteria

a.text: Alex glances up just long enough to watch her go, then back down at the sketchbook.

b.text: Oh—yeah, no, totally, the more the merrier, honestly.
I mean. I don't really know them? But sure.
Cool. I'll go ask.
She crosses the hall. Alex's pencil stops moving before she even reaches the bench.

c.text: Oh—yeah, no worries, totally. Catch you at lunch?
c.emotion: a little thrown, smooths it over

c.text: Jordan disappears into the current of the hallway. Alex still hasn't looked up.

options.text: Change the subject without making it a thing
"I think their art's actually really good, honestly."

a.text: You laugh — half a second late, the way you always do, checking if it's safe first.
See, she gets it.
Something in your stomach doesn't laugh along with the rest of you.

b.text: So wait, is it always this loud in here, or is today special?
Oh, today's tame. You should see Fridays.
The joke just sort of hangs there, unfinished. Nobody seems to mind. You do, a little.

c.text: A half-beat of quiet — the table recalibrating.
Oh, sure. Didn't say it wasn't. Just said he does it alone. A lot.

c.action: suddenly very busy with his tray

c.text: You feel Priya's eyes on you for one second longer than comfortable.

options.text: Ask Alex direct questions, pull them into the conversation
Let the conversation flow naturally, don't single them out

a.text: What do you draw, mostly?
...Comics, mostly. Stuff that isn't real.
So, basically the opposite of lunch right now.
a.emotion: one beat late, testing a joke

a.text: Ha—wait, that's actually good.
Alex's shoulders drop, just slightly. Not relaxed. Less braced.

b.text: The conversation moves on without circling back to Alex, who says nothing, and seems almost relieved not to be asked.
You're not sure if leaving them alone is kindness or just easier.
...It's fine either way.
They open the sketchbook a crack, like a door left unlocked, just in case.

options.text: Commit fully to this — stop worrying about the 'popular' crowd
Try to bridge both worlds — invite Alex to meet Jordan's group later

a.text: Honestly? I don't really care where the 'cool' table is today.
...You say that now.
I'll say it tomorrow too. Ask me again.
...Might.

b.text: You know, Jordan's actually pretty nice. You could come sit with us sometime, if you wanted.
...Maybe.
I've heard that before. The 'sometime' part usually doesn't happen.
Fair. I'll actually ask, though. Not just saying it.
...We'll see.

options.text: Warmly suggest meeting up after school
Keep it strictly business, minimal small talk

a.text: Hey — want to figure out a time to meet up? I'm free after school, if that works.
Yeah. Okay. Library?
Library.
...Okay.

b.text: We should probably just split up the sections. I can do the research part.
Fine by me.
I'll email you the outline.
b.action: zips bag the rest of the way

b.text: Alex is gone before the bell even finishes ringing.

options.text: Go to the party, text Alex to reschedule
Skip the party, keep your word to Alex
Make a quick appearance at the party, then rush to Alex's after

a.text: hey, something came up tonight, can we push to tomorrow?
The reply takes a long time to come.
Sure.
One word. You've used that word yourself, exact same way, meaning the opposite.

b.text: can't tonight, already told someone I'd help with something. next time though!
no worries!! honestly respect it, see you tmrw 🙌
Small relief, and a smaller ache — the party sounds fun. You'll never actually know how much you missed.

c.text: can do like 30 min, then I gotta run — cool?
YES honestly even 10 minutes, see you soon!!
Half a plan is still a plan. Probably.

options.text: Take the lead confidently, and make sure to highlight Alex's specific contributions
Let nerves take over, rush through your part
Step back, let Alex shine, support quietly

a.text: So we focused on migration patterns — and honestly, most of the visuals you're about to see, Alex did. They're the reason this doesn't look like a school project.
...I mean. It's not that big a deal.
It's kind of a big deal.
A few kids actually lean forward for the visuals. Alex stands a little straighter.

b.text: Okay so basically we did migration patterns and, um, the whole— here, this part, and then—
You barely notice you've stepped in front of Alex until they've gone quiet behind you.
I also wanted to mention—
—and that's basically the whole thing, thanks!
Alex doesn't try again.

c.text: Alex actually did most of the research on this — I think they should walk you through it.
So — migration patterns are basically just animals doing the smart version of what we're all doing right now, which is leaving when things get bad.
A ripple of actual laughter — not the mocking kind. Alex blinks, thrown by it, then keeps going, steadier.

lines.text: A month ago you'd have laughed at the joke about him. Today you made sure the whole class saw what he could actually do.
...You used to sit with Priya's table. Every day.
Yeah. Table's still there. Turns out it wasn't the only option.
...Noted.
You think about the lunch table that first week — Alex stiff at the edge of it, Priya's eyebrow raised but never quite objecting.
...Guess bringing me to that table wasn't the worst idea you've had.
Jury's still out on Priya. But yeah. Not the worst.
...You picked the bench on day one. Before you knew any of this would matter.
I didn't pick it because I knew. I picked it because it felt right.
...That's kind of the whole point, isn't it.
I noticed what you did up there — sharing the credit. That's harder than people think. Good.
You'll take that. You'll take all of it.
You spent weeks at the table where nobody had to slow down for anyone. Guess you never learned how.
Priya, passing in the hall, gives you a look that's almost sympathy. Almost.
You tried to hold both worlds at once, and today, in the one moment that actually counted, you dropped the one that mattered more.
Jordan catches your eye across the room, unsure whether to say something or let it go. He lets it go.
You picked the quiet bench that first day. You picked the loud version of yourself today, when it counted most.
All those weeks of trust, undone in about ninety seconds of you talking too fast to notice.
Grades don't measure everything, Sol.
No. Apparently not.
...Didn't expect you to hand this one to me. Not after where you started the year.
Yeah, well. I've had some time to figure out what actually matters.
...You've been trying to hold two things all year. This is the first time it actually worked.
Guess I finally picked the right thing to lead with.
...This isn't that different from the bench, honestly. You just let me talk.
Turns out that was the whole trick the whole time.
Confidence doesn't always look loud. Worth remembering.
You'll take quiet strength. It fits better than you expected.

voiceNotes: Sol splits between a bright, slightly-too-quick 'performing' register around Jordan/Priya (short punchy deflections, humor as armor) and a slower, more honest register with Alex, marked by trailing sentences and the ring-twist beat before anything vulnerable. Her Spanish/abuela lines are reserved for moments of real emotional stakes, never dropped casually — they're the tell that she's stopped performing. Jordan never finishes a tense beat; he always has a reframe or a hand on someone's shoulder to keep momentum going, which makes his silences (when they do happen, e.g. scene-7b) land harder. Priya's cruelty is engineered for laughs, half-beat timing like a comedian, and she never fully drops the performance except in flickers ('Didn't they?'). Alex is the most withholding voice — three-word answers as a wall, sketchbook deflections instead of eye contact, and the bag-zipping mannerism as a literal exit strategy woven into blocking, not just described. Ms. Chen's dialogue is intentionally sparse; she should almost always have the least lines in any scene she's in, and every line should feel like it was chosen, not spoken freely.

alternatives: Guess I'll hold it down, then.
I'll try not to topple the whole ecosystem.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Keeps Sol's deflective humor register but offers a slightly less structured/joke-forward option if 'structural integrity' reads too clever for a nervous first-day line.

alternatives: Nobody really notices you if you're quiet enough. Which — apparently, mission accomplished.
Nobody really notices you if you don't make noise. Working as intended, I guess.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Alternate phrasing preserves Alex's flat self-protective irony while varying the rhythm depending on how dry vs. wounded the read should land.

alternatives: —yeah, that's pretty much it, thank you!
—so, migration, that's the gist, thanks everyone!
alternativeLines.reasoning: Offers slightly different nervous-rush phrasing while keeping the accidental talk-over intact for the hollow branch.
